theme: The Dashboard Knows You — Make It Personal, Make It Urgent, Make It Sing

ideas.title: Worker Focus Panel — Click the Floor, See the Card
ideas.description: Right now the Office tab and the Dashboard tab are completely isolated worlds. You see a character bobbing at a desk but you have no idea their progress is at 14% and their task says 'unblock auth migration'. Fix it: clicking any character in the office view triggers a slide-up overlay panel (max 320px wide, anchored bottom-right of the office floor) showing that worker's full card — progress bar, task, status badge, duration. Clicking elsewhere or pressing Escape dismisses it. The panel is populated from the same workers array already in scope; this is zero new data fetching. WHY: The Office tab currently is scenic decoration. One click handler makes it operational. This is the highest ROI architectural change on the board — it turns the prettiest view into the most useful one.

ideas.title: Error Urgency Halo — Make Crises Impossible to Miss
ideas.description: Workers in 'error' state look almost identical to working workers in the office view. Add three stacked signals: (1) a slow-pulse crimson box-shadow halo behind their avatar (using the existing standby-pulse keyframe pattern but red and more intense), (2) a tiny red '!' badge absolutely positioned at the top-right of the .char__avatar circle, rendered as a 12px SVG circle with a white exclamation mark, (3) a 10% opacity red overlay on the desk element they occupy. The desk tint is a background-color on the .desk element when its occupant has status='error'. This requires passing error state down from deskOccupants which already carries the worker object. WHY: Errors are the highest-urgency state on the board. The office should alarm you. Right now a full-team error looks identical to a full-team working state at a distance. That is a dashboard safety failure.

ideas.title: Progress Micro-Sparkline — Show Momentum, Not Just Position
ideas.description: Store the last 8 progress readings per worker in a useRef map keyed by worker.id, updated on every sync cycle. Render as a tiny SVG polyline (60×10px) laid translucently behind the progress bar fill, visible as a faint trail. A flat line means the worker has been stuck at the same % for the past 8 polls. A steep line means sprint. A downward spike means something regressed. The sparkline data clears when a worker is replaced (id changes). WHY: progress% is a snapshot. Two workers both at 60% are invisible as distinct cases today — one has climbed 30 points in the last 4 minutes, one has flatlined for 20. Sparklines show momentum. Operators shouldn't need to watch a number; they should be able to see the shape of progress.

ideas.title: Office Weather Widget — Ambient Team Climate in the Floor
ideas.description: In the top-left of the office floor, replace the static '.office-sign' text label with a compact animated SVG weather widget (48×48px) that computes 'office climate' from live worker state: ☀️ sunny with rotating rays (all working, zero errors), ⛈️ lightning bolt + falling rain drops (any errors), 🌤️ sun behind a cloud (mixed active/idle), 🌫️ soft fog waves (empty / after-hours). All states are implemented as SVG elements inside the widget with their own short CSS keyframe loops (rays rotate 360° in 8s, rain drops translate-Y in 1.2s, fog drifts left in 4s). The widget is purely decorative but diegetically meaningful — it's the literal weather of the workspace. WHY: The Team Vibe Meter on the Dashboard is a badge. The Weather Widget in the Office is an environment. One you read; the other you feel. Both are needed, and neither does the other's job.

ideas.title: Task Intent Auto-Tags — Scan the Grid in One Second
ideas.description: Parse each worker's task string with a regex battery and auto-apply colored chip tags beneath the task text on WorkerCard. Tag definitions: 'fix|bug|hotfix|broken|error' → 🔴 Bug (red chip), 'deploy|ship|release|publish|ci|cd|pipeline' → 🟠 Ship (orange), 'design|ui|ux|css|layout|frontend|component' → 🟣 Design (purple), 'test|qa|spec|review|coverage|lint' → 🔵 QA (blue), 'data|migration|schema|db|database|query' → 🟤 Data (brown), 'docs|readme|changelog|write' → 🟢 Docs (green). A task can match multiple tags. Chips are small rounded-pill spans (font-size 0.65rem, padding 2px 8px). Zero backend work — pure client-side string analysis. WHY: All task text is undifferentiated prose today. Tags let you scan the workers grid and immediately perceive 'this is a bugfix sprint' or 'deployment day' without reading a single sentence.

ideas.title: Sync Streak Counter — Make Uptime Feel Like an Achievement
ideas.description: Track consecutive successful sync cycles in a useRef integer (increment on both-fetches-success in syncFromGitHub, reset to 0 on any error). Persist the all-time streak best to localStorage under 'bloberto-best-streak'. Display below the sync time in the Live stat card as '🔗 47 syncs' in 0.65rem muted text. When the current streak beats the stored best, the sync stat card briefly flashes a green border for 1.5s (add/remove a 'streak-best' CSS class). WHY: Reliability is invisible until it breaks. A streak counter makes the polling engine feel alive and trustworthy rather than silent infrastructure. A new personal best is a tiny celebration that costs nothing to build but earns disproportionate affection from anyone who sees it happen.

ideas.title: Roll Call Easter Egg — All Characters Wave at Once
ideas.description: Clicking the '🏢 Bloberto's HQ' sign in the top-left of the office floor (add pointer-events: auto to .office-sign) triggers a 'rollcall' mode: all .char elements simultaneously receive a CSS class 'char--rollcall' for 900ms, which plays a hop-and-wave keyframe (translateY -12px at 40%, then a brief rotate ±15deg, back to baseline). During the animation, Bloberto's speech bubble briefly shows 'Alright team — sound off! 📣' without requiring a hover. Implemented as a single setTimeout that adds then removes the class. No state needed — a ref suffices. WHY: Easter eggs are the signature of a product made with love. One unexpected moment of collective joy is worth more than a feature in a changelog. It's the kind of thing someone records and shares in Slack.

ideas.title: Keyboard Command Palette — ⌘K for Power Users
ideas.description: Pressing ⌘K (or Ctrl+K on Windows/Linux) opens a minimal floating command palette modal centered on screen. Commands available: 'Switch to Office', 'Switch to Dashboard', 'Toggle dark/light theme', 'Refresh data now', 'Copy current status to clipboard' (formats a plain-text team summary: 'N active, N idle, vibe: X'). Arrow keys navigate the list, Enter executes, Escape closes. The modal is a simple <dialog> element with a short CSS slide-down animation. The command list is a static array of {label, action} objects — adding new commands is two lines. WHY: Power users who embed this dashboard on a second monitor shouldn't need a mouse for routine actions. A command palette also signals that this is a serious, keyboard-first product. It takes half a day to build and permanently elevates the perceived quality of the tool.

ideas.title: Live Roster Presence Strip — See Who, Not Just How Many
ideas.description: Insert a compact horizontal chip strip between the StatsBar and the section header on the Dashboard tab. Every roster member gets a 32×32px avatar chip with a colored presence ring: solid green ring (working), pulsing yellow ring (idle), dashed grey ring (offline/ghost), blinking red ring (error). Chips are ordered: working first, then idle, then offline. Each chip has a tooltip on hover showing their current task or away message. Clicking an offline chip scrolls to their ghost desk in the office view (or shows a mini popover with their away message from the Ghost Sticky Notes idea). The data source is the existing allWorkers array + roster array already in App state — no new fetching. WHY: The StatsBar tells you '3 active' — a number. The Presence Strip tells you 'Carlos, Maya, and Dave are active' — a team. Aggregate metrics erase individuals. This restores the human layer of the data without adding a single API call.

hire_proposals.role: Developer Experience Engineer / Data Pipeline Owner
hire_proposals.reason: The dashboard is a read-only window into JSON files that someone updates manually (or via bots we can't see). There is no write path, no schema contract, no tooling for the team to update their own status. A DX hire would own: the workers.json + activity.json schema and versioning, the CLI or webhook that bots use to push state changes, validation that prevents malformed data from breaking the UI, and potentially a lightweight admin panel for manual overrides. Without this role, every new dashboard feature is blocked by the fragility of the data layer. Every idea in Review 1 and Review 2 renders correctly only as long as the JSON is well-formed. That's a single point of failure for a product that's becoming a real team artifact.
